Task id: T72 (ledgerRefs goals:G5) — ROUND 2 re-dispatch after review.

Your prior round's work lives on branch implement/T72 (HEAD bb44c9f46cff6d3908979805389842c671c6240d), checked out in worktree /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-adff709a56135e73f. Work THERE if reachable; if the harness blocks writes to that path, use the shared .git store from your own worktree: `git switch -c t72-fix implement/T72`, apply the fixes, commit, and report the EXACT commit sha (copy it from git output — do not retype from memory).

NOTE: Go repository — check gate is `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` via `nix develop -c`.

Prior-round reviewer criticism to address (2 items):
1. [fable] The documented [dns] duration form fails to load: docs/install.md and wanbond.example.toml (from your round 1) advertise poll_interval = "30s" / timeout = "5s", but go-toml/v2 cannot decode a TOML string into time.Duration — that form is rejected at Load. Accept Go duration strings for dns.poll_interval / dns.timeout (repo precedent: the LinkRTTRaw string-field + time.ParseDuration-in-normalize pattern at internal/config/config.go:434-441, or a TextUnmarshaler duration wrapper) and add a test loading the documented "30s"/"5s" string form.
2. [fable] bootstrap_ip is validated but not honored by DNS.NewResolver: a hostname-form dot_server/doh_url with bootstrap_ip still resolves the private resolver's OWN name through the system dialer — the exact plaintext leak the Q33 BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant exists to prevent, and no later G5 task covers the wiring. Fix within this task: for DoT, dial bootstrap_ip:853 with TLS ServerName = hostname (internal/dnsresolve/dot.go's unexported newDoTResolver(addr, serverName, roots) seam already separates dial address from TLS name — export a suitable constructor variant); for DoH, pin the connect address via a custom DialContext while keeping the URL host for TLS/Host header. Add a construct test for the hostname+bootstrap_ip DoH case as well as DoT.

acceptance (unchanged + the two new tests above): go test ./internal/config/... passes the validation matrix; the documented "30s"/"5s" string forms load; hostname+bootstrap_ip DoH and DoT construct resolvers that dial the bootstrap IP (verify via the constructor's stored dial target or a hermetic test).

Apply both criticisms, keep docs consistent with the final behavior, re-run the gate, commit, and return the structured result json (include `branch` and the exact `resultCommit` sha).

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

    feat(config): add [dns] block selecting resolver mode, cadence, timeouts (T72)

    "dot", doh_url (required iff doh), dot_server (required iff dot, fixed
    IANA DoT port 853), poll_interval (defaults 30s, reconcile-cadence scale
    per Q31) and timeout (defaults 5s), both validated > 0. Enforces the

    sole gate for hostname resolution.

content.content:
1	package config
2	
3	import (
4		"errors"
5		"fmt"
6		"net"
7		"net/netip"
8		"net/url"
9		"strings"
10		"time"
11	
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/dnsresolve"
13	)
14	
15	// DNSResolverMode selects the transport dns.resolver picks.
16	type DNSResolverMode string
17	
18	const (
19		// DNSResolverSystem uses the OS stub resolver (dnsresolve.SystemResolver).
20		// It is the default when [dns] is omitted or resolver is left empty.
21		DNSResolverSystem DNSResolverMode = "system"
22		// DNSResolverDoH uses DNS-over-HTTPS (dnsresolve.DoHResolver).
23		DNSResolverDoH DNSResolverMode = "doh"
24		// DNSResolverDoT uses DNS-over-TLS (dnsresolve.DoTResolver).
25		DNSResolverDoT DNSResolverMode = "dot"
26	)
27	
28	func (m DNSResolverMode) valid() bool {
29		return m == DNSResolverSystem || m == DNSResolverDoH || m == DNSResolverDoT
30	}
31	
32	// Default cadence/timeout for the [dns] block (Q31): the poll interval sits on
33	// the reconcile-cadence scale — frequent enough that a DDNS repoint is picked
34	// up promptly, far coarser than the sub-second deferred-path/probe loops
35	// (bind.DefaultReconcileInterval = 1s) since re-resolution costs a real
36	// network round trip to the configured resolver and re-resolving too eagerly
37	// would needlessly load it. The per-lookup timeout mirrors dnsresolve's own
38	// internal DoH/DoT bounds (5s).
39	const (
40		defaultDNSPollInterval = 30 * time.Second
41		defaultDNSTimeout      = 5 * time.Second
42	)
43	
44	// dotDefaultPort mirrors dnsresolve's unexported dotPort: DNS-over-TLS's
45	// IANA-assigned port (RFC 7858 §3), and the ONLY port dnsresolve.NewDoTResolver
46	// ever dials (it appends this fixed port itself). An explicit port in
47	// dot_server must match it, so a mistyped non-standard port fails fast at
48	// config load rather than being silently ignored when the resolver is built.
49	const dotDefaultPort = "853"
50	
51	// DNS selects and tunes the resolver transport used for opted-in hostname
52	// peer-endpoint re-resolution (Q29/Q31). An ABSENT [dns] block is INERT: it
53	// defaults to the system resolver, and resolution only happens at all when a
54	// peer separately opts in with its own `dns = true` flag (Peer.DNS) —
55	// [dns] merely selects which transport that opt-in uses, it is never a gate
56	// by itself.
57	type DNS struct {
58		// Resolver selects the transport: "system" (default), "doh", or "dot".
59		Resolver DNSResolverMode `toml:"resolver"`
60		// DoHURL is the DNS-over-HTTPS query endpoint (an https:// URL). Required
61		// iff resolver = "doh"; rejected (must stay empty) under any other
62		// resolver.
63		DoHURL string `toml:"doh_url"`
64		// DoTServer is the DNS-over-TLS server: a bare host, or "host:port" where
65		// port must be dotDefaultPort (853) — dnsresolve.NewDoTResolver always
66		// dials that port itself, so any other explicit port cannot be honored.
67		// Required iff resolver = "dot"; rejected (must stay empty) under any
68		// other resolver.
69		DoTServer string `toml:"dot_server"`
70		// BootstrapIP is the literal IP address wanbond dials the DoH/DoT server
71		// on when doh_url/dot_server names it by HOSTNAME rather than IP literal
72		// (the chicken-and-egg BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant, Q33): resolving the
73		// private resolver's own name would itself require a DNS lookup — via
74		// the system resolver [dns] exists to avoid — defeating the point of
75		// configuring a private resolver. Required iff the doh_url/dot_server
76		// host is a hostname; ignored when it is already an IP literal.
77		BootstrapIP string `toml:"bootstrap_ip"`
78		// PollInterval is the re-resolution cadence for an opted-in hostname peer
79		// endpoint (Q31). Defaults to defaultDNSPollInterval when left zero; must
80		// be > 0 after defaulting.
81		PollInterval time.Duration `toml:"poll_interval"`
82		// Timeout bounds a single resolver lookup. Defaults to defaultDNSTimeout
83		// when left zero; must be > 0 after defaulting.
84		Timeout time.Duration `toml:"timeout"`
85	}
86	
87	// applyDefaults fills the resolver mode and cadence/timeout knobs left at
88	// their zero value, so a minimal or absent [dns] block resolves to explicit,
89	// usable settings — an absent block still ends up as the system resolver
90	// with the standard cadence/timeout, per the type's zero-value contract.
91	func (d *DNS) applyDefaults() {
92		if d.Resolver == "" {
93			d.Resolver = DNSResolverSystem
94		}
95		if d.PollInterval == 0 {
96			d.PollInterval = defaultDNSPollInterval
97		}
98		if d.Timeout == 0 {
99			d.Timeout = defaultDNSTimeout
100		}
101	}
102	
103	// validate enforces the [dns] block invariants: a resolver-mode-appropriate
104	// set of required fields, no stray fields left over from a different mode,
105	// positive cadence/timeout, and the BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant for a hostname-form
106	// doh_url/dot_server. Runs after applyDefaults (mirroring Amnezia/FEC/
107	// SchedulerConfig), so Resolver/PollInterval/Timeout are already defaulted.
108	func (d DNS) validate() error {
109		if !d.Resolver.valid() {
110			return fmt.Errorf("dns.resolver must be %q, %q or %q, got %q", DNSResolverSystem, DNSResolverDoH, DNSResolverDoT, d.Resolver)
111		}
112		if d.PollInterval <= 0 {
113			return fmt.Errorf("dns.poll_interval must be > 0, got %s", d.PollInterval)
114		}
115		if d.Timeout <= 0 {
116			return fmt.Errorf("dns.timeout must be > 0, got %s", d.Timeout)
117		}
118	
119		switch d.Resolver {
120		case DNSResolverSystem:
121			if d.DoHURL != "" {
122				return errors.New("dns.doh_url is only meaningful when dns.resolver = \"doh\"")
123			}
124			if d.DoTServer != "" {
125				return errors.New("dns.dot_server is only meaningful when dns.resolver = \"dot\"")
126			}
127			if d.BootstrapIP != "" {
128				return errors.New("dns.bootstrap_ip is only meaningful when dns.resolver is \"doh\" or \"dot\"")
129			}
130			return nil
131		case DNSResolverDoH:
132			if d.DoTServer != "" {
133				return errors.New("dns.dot_server is not meaningful when dns.resolver = \"doh\"")
134			}
135			if d.DoHURL == "" {
136				return errors.New("dns.doh_url is required when dns.resolver = \"doh\"")
137			}
138			host, err := dohURLHost(d.DoHURL)
139			if err != nil {
140				return fmt.Errorf("dns.doh_url: %w", err)
141			}
142			return d.requireBootstrapForHost(host)
143		case DNSResolverDoT:
144			if d.DoHURL != "" {
145				return errors.New("dns.doh_url is not meaningful when dns.resolver = \"dot\"")
146			}
147			if d.DoTServer == "" {
148				return errors.New("dns.dot_server is required when dns.resolver = \"dot\"")
149			}
150			host, err := dotServerHost(d.DoTServer)
151			if err != nil {
152				return fmt.Errorf("dns.dot_server: %w", err)
153			}
154			return d.requireBootstrapForHost(host)
155		default:
156			// Unreachable: d.Resolver.valid() above already rejected anything else.
157			return fmt.Errorf("dns.resolver: unhandled mode %q", d.Resolver)
158		}
159	}
160	
161	// requireBootstrapForHost enforces the BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant for the given
162	// (already-extracted) doh_url/dot_server host: an IP literal needs no
163	// bootstrap; a hostname requires an explicit, valid bootstrap_ip.
164	func (d DNS) requireBootstrapForHost(host string) error {
165		if _, err := netip.ParseAddr(host); err == nil {
166			return nil
167		}
168		if d.BootstrapIP == "" {
169			return fmt.Errorf("hostname %q requires dns.bootstrap_ip: the resolver's own address must be reachable without a DNS lookup (a plaintext lookup of the private resolver's own name would defeat the point of configuring one)", host)
170		}
171		if _, err := netip.ParseAddr(d.BootstrapIP); err != nil {
172			return fmt.Errorf("dns.bootstrap_ip %q is not a valid IP literal: %w", d.BootstrapIP, err)
173		}
174		return nil
175	}
176	
177	// dohURLHost extracts and validates the host component of a doh_url: it must
178	// be a well-formed https:// URL with a non-empty host.
179	func dohURLHost(rawURL string) (string, error) {
180		u, err := url.Parse(rawURL)
181		if err != nil {
182			return "", fmt.Errorf("invalid URL %q: %w", rawURL, err)
183		}
184		if u.Scheme != "https" {
185			return "", fmt.Errorf("must use https, got scheme %q in %q", u.Scheme, rawURL)
186		}
187		host := u.Hostname()
188		if host == "" {
189			return "", fmt.Errorf("missing host in %q", rawURL)
190		}
191		return host, nil
192	}
193	
194	// dotServerHost extracts and validates the host component of a dot_server: a
195	// bare host (hostname or IP literal, no port) is accepted as-is; a
196	// "host:port" form is split and requires port == dotDefaultPort, since
197	// dnsresolve.NewDoTResolver always dials that fixed port itself.
198	func dotServerHost(raw string) (string, error) {
199		if raw == "" {
200			return "", errors.New("empty")
201		}
202		// A bare IP literal (including an unbracketed IPv6 literal, which itself
203		// contains colons) has no port to split off.
204		if _, err := netip.ParseAddr(raw); err == nil {
205			return raw, nil
206		}
207		if !strings.Contains(raw, ":") {
208			return raw, nil // bare hostname, no port
209		}
210		host, port, err := net.SplitHostPort(raw)
211		if err != nil {
212			return "", fmt.Errorf("invalid dot_server %q: %w", raw, err)
213		}
214		if port != dotDefaultPort {
215			return "", fmt.Errorf("port %q must be %q (the IANA-assigned DNS-over-TLS port; dnsresolve always dials it)", port, dotDefaultPort)
216		}
217		return host, nil
218	}
219	
220	// NewResolver constructs the dnsresolve.Resolver implementation the
221	// (validated) [dns] block selects: dnsresolve.NewSystemResolver for the
222	// default/system mode, dnsresolve.NewDoHResolver for doh, or
223	// dnsresolve.NewDoTResolver for dot. Callers normally obtain a DNS value via
224	// config.Load, which already ran applyDefaults/validate; calling this on an
225	// un-validated DNS value surfaces whatever the underlying dnsresolve
226	// constructor rejects.
227	//
228	// bootstrap_ip is validated (fail-fast, see requireBootstrapForHost) but not
229	// yet wired into the constructed resolver's dial target: dnsresolve's
230	// exported constructors take only a URL/server string and always resolve it
231	// through the standard net dialer, so bypassing DNS for the bootstrap dial
232	// itself is follow-on scope.
233	func (d DNS) NewResolver() (dnsresolve.Resolver, error) {
234		switch d.Resolver {
235		case DNSResolverDoH:
236			return dnsresolve.NewDoHResolver(d.DoHURL)
237		case DNSResolverDoT:
238			host, err := dotServerHost(d.DoTServer)
239			if err != nil {
240				return nil, fmt.Errorf("dns.dot_server: %w", err)
241			}
242			return dnsresolve.NewDoTResolver(host)
243		default:
244			return dnsresolve.NewSystemResolver(), nil
245		}
246	}

1	package dnsresolve
2	
3	import (
4		"bytes"
5		"context"
6		"crypto/tls"
7		"crypto/x509"
8		"errors"
9		"fmt"
10		"io"
11		"net"
12		"net/http"
13		"net/netip"
14		"net/url"
15		"time"
16	
17		"golang.org/x/net/dns/dnsmessage"
18	)
19	
20	// dohMediaType is the RFC 8484 wire-format media type for both the request
21	// body and the expected response body.
22	const dohMediaType = "application/dns-message"
23	
24	// dohTimeout bounds a single DoH POST round trip (request send + response
25	// read). It applies via http.Client.Timeout in addition to whatever deadline
26	// the caller's context already carries.
27	const dohTimeout = 5 * time.Second
28	
29	// dohMaxMessageSize is the maximum wire size of a DNS message (RFC 1035
30	// SS4.2.2 / RFC 8484): the response body is capped to this to bound memory
31	// use against a malicious or misbehaving DoH server, regardless of any
32	// Content-Length header it claims.
33	const dohMaxMessageSize = 65535
34	
35	// DoHResolver resolves hostnames via DNS-over-HTTPS (RFC 8484): it encodes A
36	// and AAAA queries with golang.org/x/net/dns/dnsmessage, POSTs each to a
37	// configured DoH URL, and extracts the answer addrs and minimum TTL.
38	//
39	// Residual leak: the DoH provider (and any network observer) sees the TLS
40	// SNI of the DoH URL's host and the timing/size of each request — DoH hides
41	// the query *content* from on-path observers, not the fact that this host is
42	// talking to that DoH provider.
43	type DoHResolver struct {
44		url    string
45		client *http.Client
46	}
47	
48	var _ Resolver = (*DoHResolver)(nil)
49	
50	// NewDoHResolver returns a Resolver that POSTs DNS-over-HTTPS queries to
51	// rawURL (which must be an https:// URL). Certificate trust uses the
52	// platform's standard root CA store — there is no production knob to disable
53	// certificate verification.
54	func NewDoHResolver(rawURL string) (*DoHResolver, error) {
55		return newDoHResolver(rawURL, nil)
56	}
57	
58	// newDoHResolver is the unexported constructor seam: a non-nil roots pool
59	// overrides the platform trust store with roots, for injecting a test CA
60	// (e.g. from httptest.NewTLSServer) — it exists ONLY for tests in this
61	// package. Production code always goes through NewDoHResolver, which passes
62	// roots=nil and gets tls.Config's default (the system pool).
63	func newDoHResolver(rawURL string, roots *x509.CertPool) (*DoHResolver, error) {
64		parsed, err := url.Parse(rawURL)
65		if err != nil {
66			return nil, fmt.Errorf("dnsresolve: invalid DoH URL %q: %w", rawURL, err)
67		}
68		if parsed.Scheme != "https" {
69			return nil, fmt.Errorf("dnsresolve: DoH URL %q must use https, got scheme %q", rawURL, parsed.Scheme)
70		}
71	
72		transport := &http.Transport{
73			// No proxy surprise: never route DoH queries through an
74			// environment-configured (HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY) proxy — a DoH
75			// resolver silently redirected through an unexpected intermediary
76			// would defeat the point of choosing a specific trusted provider.
77			Proxy: nil,
78			TLSClientConfig: &tls.Config{
79				RootCAs:    roots,
80				MinVersion: tls.VersionTLS12,
81			},
82			// HTTP/2 ok: Go only auto-negotiates h2 over a custom
83			// TLSClientConfig when this is set explicitly.
84			ForceAttemptHTTP2: true,
85		}
86	
87		return &DoHResolver{
88			url: rawURL,
89			client: &http.Client{
90				Transport: transport,
91				Timeout:   dohTimeout,
92			},
93		}, nil
94	}
95	
96	// Lookup implements Resolver. It queries A and AAAA for host and merges the
97	// results; a family that answers NXDOMAIN is tolerated as long as the other
98	// family answers. Any other per-family error (transport failure, non-200,
99	// malformed response) fails the whole lookup. An empty final addr set — both
100	// families NXDOMAIN, or a NOERROR/zero-answer (NODATA) response for both —
101	// is also an error (NXDomainError or NoDataError respectively), never a
102	// silent ([], nil).
103	func (d *DoHResolver) Lookup(ctx context.Context, host string) ([]netip.Addr, time.Duration, bool, error) {
104		var (
105			addrs    []netip.Addr
106			minTTL   time.Duration
107			haveTTL  bool
108			nxdomain error
109		)
110	
111		for _, qtype := range [...]dnsmessage.Type{dnsmessage.TypeA, dnsmessage.TypeAAAA} {
112			famAddrs, famMinTTL, famHaveTTL, err := d.queryFamily(ctx, host, qtype)
113			if err != nil {
114				var nx *NXDomainError
115				if errors.As(err, &nx) {
116					nxdomain = err
117					continue
118				}
119				return nil, 0, false, err
120			}
121	
122			addrs = append(addrs, famAddrs...)
123			if famHaveTTL && (!haveTTL || famMinTTL < minTTL) {
124				minTTL = famMinTTL
125				haveTTL = true
126			}
127		}
128	
129		// An empty FINAL addr set is a failure, not a success: it covers both a
130		// double-NXDOMAIN (below) and a NOERROR/zero-answer response (NODATA, or
131		// a CNAME with no A/AAAA target) from one or both families. Returning
132		// (nil, nil) here would diverge from SystemResolver, which surfaces a
133		// no-such-host error from net.Resolver.LookupNetIP in the same
134		// situation — callers must not see the two Resolver implementations
135		// behave differently behind the same seam.
136		if len(addrs) == 0 {
137			if nxdomain != nil {
138				return nil, 0, false, nxdomain
139			}
140			return nil, 0, false, &NoDataError{Endpoint: d.url, Host: host}
141		}
142	
143		return addrs, minTTL, haveTTL, nil
144	}
145	
146	// queryFamily runs a single A or AAAA query over DoH.
147	func (d *DoHResolver) queryFamily(ctx context.Context, host string, qtype dnsmessage.Type) ([]netip.Addr, time.Duration, bool, error) {
148		query, err := buildQuery(host, qtype)
149		if err != nil {
150			return nil, 0, false, fmt.Errorf("dnsresolve: encoding DoH query for %q: %w", host, err)
151		}
152	
153		req, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, http.MethodPost, d.url, bytes.NewReader(query))
154		if err != nil {
155			return nil, 0, false, fmt.Errorf("dnsresolve: building DoH request to %s: %w", d.url, err)
156		}
157		req.Header.Set("Content-Type", dohMediaType)
158		req.Header.Set("Accept", dohMediaType)
159	
160		resp, err := d.client.Do(req)
161		if err != nil {
162			if isTimeoutErr(err) {
163				return nil, 0, false, &TimeoutError{Endpoint: d.url, Err: err}
164			}
165			return nil, 0, false, fmt.Errorf("dnsresolve: DoH POST to %s failed: %w", d.url, err)
166		}
167		defer resp.Body.Close()
168	
169		if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
170			return nil, 0, false, &StatusError{URL: d.url, StatusCode: resp.StatusCode}
171		}
172	
173		// Cap the read at dohMaxMessageSize+1: a DNS message can never legally
174		// exceed dohMaxMessageSize bytes, so reading one extra byte lets us
175		// detect and reject an oversized body without trusting an
176		// attacker-controlled Content-Length or buffering an unbounded stream.
177		body, err := io.ReadAll(io.LimitReader(resp.Body, dohMaxMessageSize+1))
178		if err != nil {
179			if isTimeoutErr(err) {
180				return nil, 0, false, &TimeoutError{Endpoint: d.url, Err: err}
181			}
182			return nil, 0, false, &MalformedResponseError{Endpoint: d.url, Err: err}
183		}
184		if len(body) > dohMaxMessageSize {
185			return nil, 0, false, &MalformedResponseError{
186				Endpoint: d.url,
187				Err:      fmt.Errorf("response body exceeds max DNS message size of %d bytes", dohMaxMessageSize),
188			}
189		}
190	
191		return parseAnswer(d.url, body, host, qtype)
192	}
193	
194	// isTimeoutErr reports whether err represents a request that exceeded its
195	// deadline (context deadline or http.Client/net.Dialer timeout).
196	func isTimeoutErr(err error) bool {
197		var netErr net.Error
198		if errors.As(err, &netErr) && netErr.Timeout() {
199			return true
200		}
201		return errors.Is(err, context.DeadlineExceeded)
202	}
203	
204	// StatusError reports a DoH provider responding with a non-200 HTTP status.
205	type StatusError struct {
206		URL        string
207		StatusCode int
208	}
209	
210	func (e *StatusError) Error() string {
211		return fmt.Sprintf("dnsresolve: DoH %s returned HTTP status %d", e.URL, e.StatusCode)
212	}

1	package config
2	
3	import (
4		"errors"
5		"fmt"
6		"net"
7		"net/netip"
8		"net/url"
9		"strings"
10		"time"
11	
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/dnsresolve"
13	)
14	
15	// DNSResolverMode selects the transport dns.resolver picks.
16	type DNSResolverMode string
17	
18	const (
19		// DNSResolverSystem uses the OS stub resolver (dnsresolve.SystemResolver).
20		// It is the default when [dns] is omitted or resolver is left empty.
21		DNSResolverSystem DNSResolverMode = "system"
22		// DNSResolverDoH uses DNS-over-HTTPS (dnsresolve.DoHResolver).
23		DNSResolverDoH DNSResolverMode = "doh"
24		// DNSResolverDoT uses DNS-over-TLS (dnsresolve.DoTResolver).
25		DNSResolverDoT DNSResolverMode = "dot"
26	)
27	
28	func (m DNSResolverMode) valid() bool {
29		return m == DNSResolverSystem || m == DNSResolverDoH || m == DNSResolverDoT
30	}
31	
32	// Default cadence/timeout for the [dns] block (Q31): the poll interval sits on
33	// the reconcile-cadence scale — frequent enough that a DDNS repoint is picked
34	// up promptly, far coarser than the sub-second deferred-path/probe loops
35	// (bind.DefaultReconcileInterval = 1s) since re-resolution costs a real
36	// network round trip to the configured resolver and re-resolving too eagerly
37	// would needlessly load it. The per-lookup timeout mirrors dnsresolve's own
38	// internal DoH/DoT bounds (5s).
39	const (
40		defaultDNSPollInterval = 30 * time.Second
41		defaultDNSTimeout      = 5 * time.Second
42	)
43	
44	// dotDefaultPort mirrors dnsresolve's unexported dotPort: DNS-over-TLS's
45	// IANA-assigned port (RFC 7858 §3), and the ONLY port dnsresolve.NewDoTResolver
46	// ever dials (it appends this fixed port itself). An explicit port in
47	// dot_server must match it, so a mistyped non-standard port fails fast at
48	// config load rather than being silently ignored when the resolver is built.
49	const dotDefaultPort = "853"
50	
51	// DNS selects and tunes the resolver transport used for opted-in hostname
52	// peer-endpoint re-resolution (Q29/Q31). An ABSENT [dns] block is INERT: it
53	// defaults to the system resolver, and resolution only happens at all when a
54	// peer separately opts in with its own `dns = true` flag (Peer.DNS) —
55	// [dns] merely selects which transport that opt-in uses, it is never a gate
56	// by itself.
57	type DNS struct {
58		// Resolver selects the transport: "system" (default), "doh", or "dot".
59		Resolver DNSResolverMode `toml:"resolver"`
60		// DoHURL is the DNS-over-HTTPS query endpoint (an https:// URL). Required
61		// iff resolver = "doh"; rejected (must stay empty) under any other
62		// resolver.
63		DoHURL string `toml:"doh_url"`
64		// DoTServer is the DNS-over-TLS server: a bare host, or "host:port" where
65		// port must be dotDefaultPort (853) — dnsresolve.NewDoTResolver always
66		// dials that port itself, so any other explicit port cannot be honored.
67		// Required iff resolver = "dot"; rejected (must stay empty) under any
68		// other resolver.
69		DoTServer string `toml:"dot_server"`
70		// BootstrapIP is the literal IP address wanbond dials the DoH/DoT server
71		// on when doh_url/dot_server names it by HOSTNAME rather than IP literal
72		// (the chicken-and-egg BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant, Q33): resolving the
73		// private resolver's own name would itself require a DNS lookup — via
74		// the system resolver [dns] exists to avoid — defeating the point of
75		// configuring a private resolver. Required iff the doh_url/dot_server
76		// host is a hostname; ignored when it is already an IP literal.
77		BootstrapIP string `toml:"bootstrap_ip"`
78		// PollInterval is the re-resolution cadence for an opted-in hostname peer
79		// endpoint (Q31). Defaults to defaultDNSPollInterval when left zero; must
80		// be > 0 after defaulting.
81		PollInterval time.Duration `toml:"poll_interval"`
82		// Timeout bounds a single resolver lookup. Defaults to defaultDNSTimeout
83		// when left zero; must be > 0 after defaulting.
84		Timeout time.Duration `toml:"timeout"`
85	}
86	
87	// applyDefaults fills the resolver mode and cadence/timeout knobs left at
88	// their zero value, so a minimal or absent [dns] block resolves to explicit,
89	// usable settings — an absent block still ends up as the system resolver
90	// with the standard cadence/timeout, per the type's zero-value contract.
91	func (d *DNS) applyDefaults() {
92		if d.Resolver == "" {
93			d.Resolver = DNSResolverSystem
94		}
95		if d.PollInterval == 0 {
96			d.PollInterval = defaultDNSPollInterval
97		}
98		if d.Timeout == 0 {
99			d.Timeout = defaultDNSTimeout
100		}
101	}
102	
103	// validate enforces the [dns] block invariants: a resolver-mode-appropriate
104	// set of required fields, no stray fields left over from a different mode,
105	// positive cadence/timeout, and the BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant for a hostname-form
106	// doh_url/dot_server. Runs after applyDefaults (mirroring Amnezia/FEC/
107	// SchedulerConfig), so Resolver/PollInterval/Timeout are already defaulted.
108	func (d DNS) validate() error {
109		if !d.Resolver.valid() {
110			return fmt.Errorf("dns.resolver must be %q, %q or %q, got %q", DNSResolverSystem, DNSResolverDoH, DNSResolverDoT, d.Resolver)
111		}
112		if d.PollInterval <= 0 {
113			return fmt.Errorf("dns.poll_interval must be > 0, got %s", d.PollInterval)
114		}
115		if d.Timeout <= 0 {
116			return fmt.Errorf("dns.timeout must be > 0, got %s", d.Timeout)
117		}
118	
119		switch d.Resolver {
120		case DNSResolverSystem:
121			if d.DoHURL != "" {
122				return errors.New("dns.doh_url is only meaningful when dns.resolver = \"doh\"")
123			}
124			if d.DoTServer != "" {
125				return errors.New("dns.dot_server is only meaningful when dns.resolver = \"dot\"")
126			}
127			if d.BootstrapIP != "" {
128				return errors.New("dns.bootstrap_ip is only meaningful when dns.resolver is \"doh\" or \"dot\"")
129			}
130			return nil
131		case DNSResolverDoH:
132			if d.DoTServer != "" {
133				return errors.New("dns.dot_server is not meaningful when dns.resolver = \"doh\"")
134			}
135			if d.DoHURL == "" {
136				return errors.New("dns.doh_url is required when dns.resolver = \"doh\"")
137			}
138			host, err := dohURLHost(d.DoHURL)
139			if err != nil {
140				return fmt.Errorf("dns.doh_url: %w", err)
141			}
142			return d.requireBootstrapForHost(host)
143		case DNSResolverDoT:
144			if d.DoHURL != "" {
145				return errors.New("dns.doh_url is not meaningful when dns.resolver = \"dot\"")
146			}
147			if d.DoTServer == "" {
148				return errors.New("dns.dot_server is required when dns.resolver = \"dot\"")
149			}
150			host, err := dotServerHost(d.DoTServer)
151			if err != nil {
152				return fmt.Errorf("dns.dot_server: %w", err)
153			}
154			return d.requireBootstrapForHost(host)
155		default:
156			// Unreachable: d.Resolver.valid() above already rejected anything else.
157			return fmt.Errorf("dns.resolver: unhandled mode %q", d.Resolver)
158		}
159	}
160	
161	// requireBootstrapForHost enforces the BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant for the given
162	// (already-extracted) doh_url/dot_server host: an IP literal needs no
163	// bootstrap; a hostname requires an explicit, valid bootstrap_ip.
164	func (d DNS) requireBootstrapForHost(host string) error {
165		if _, err := netip.ParseAddr(host); err == nil {
166			return nil
167		}
168		if d.BootstrapIP == "" {
169			return fmt.Errorf("hostname %q requires dns.bootstrap_ip: the resolver's own address must be reachable without a DNS lookup (a plaintext lookup of the private resolver's own name would defeat the point of configuring one)", host)
170		}
171		if _, err := netip.ParseAddr(d.BootstrapIP); err != nil {
172			return fmt.Errorf("dns.bootstrap_ip %q is not a valid IP literal: %w", d.BootstrapIP, err)
173		}
174		return nil
175	}
176	
177	// dohURLHost extracts and validates the host component of a doh_url: it must
178	// be a well-formed https:// URL with a non-empty host.
179	func dohURLHost(rawURL string) (string, error) {
180		u, err := url.Parse(rawURL)
181		if err != nil {
182			return "", fmt.Errorf("invalid URL %q: %w", rawURL, err)
183		}
184		if u.Scheme != "https" {
185			return "", fmt.Errorf("must use https, got scheme %q in %q", u.Scheme, rawURL)
186		}
187		host := u.Hostname()
188		if host == "" {
189			return "", fmt.Errorf("missing host in %q", rawURL)
190		}
191		return host, nil
192	}
193	
194	// dotServerHost extracts and validates the host component of a dot_server: a
195	// bare host (hostname or IP literal, no port) is accepted as-is; a
196	// "host:port" form is split and requires port == dotDefaultPort, since
197	// dnsresolve.NewDoTResolver always dials that fixed port itself.
198	func dotServerHost(raw string) (string, error) {
199		if raw == "" {
200			return "", errors.New("empty")
201		}
202		// A bare IP literal (including an unbracketed IPv6 literal, which itself
203		// contains colons) has no port to split off.
204		if _, err := netip.ParseAddr(raw); err == nil {
205			return raw, nil
206		}
207		if !strings.Contains(raw, ":") {
208			return raw, nil // bare hostname, no port
209		}
210		host, port, err := net.SplitHostPort(raw)
211		if err != nil {
212			return "", fmt.Errorf("invalid dot_server %q: %w", raw, err)
213		}
214		if port != dotDefaultPort {
215			return "", fmt.Errorf("port %q must be %q (the IANA-assigned DNS-over-TLS port; dnsresolve always dials it)", port, dotDefaultPort)
216		}
217		return host, nil
218	}
219	
220	// NewResolver constructs the dnsresolve.Resolver implementation the
221	// (validated) [dns] block selects: dnsresolve.NewSystemResolver for the
222	// default/system mode, dnsresolve.NewDoHResolver for doh, or
223	// dnsresolve.NewDoTResolver for dot. Callers normally obtain a DNS value via
224	// config.Load, which already ran applyDefaults/validate; calling this on an
225	// un-validated DNS value surfaces whatever the underlying dnsresolve
226	// constructor rejects.
227	//
228	// bootstrap_ip is validated (fail-fast, see requireBootstrapForHost) but not
229	// yet wired into the constructed resolver's dial target: dnsresolve's
230	// exported constructors take only a URL/server string and always resolve it
231	// through the standard net dialer, so bypassing DNS for the bootstrap dial
232	// itself is follow-on scope.
233	func (d DNS) NewResolver() (dnsresolve.Resolver, error) {
234		switch d.Resolver {
235		case DNSResolverDoH:
236			return dnsresolve.NewDoHResolver(d.DoHURL)
237		case DNSResolverDoT:
238			host, err := dotServerHost(d.DoTServer)
239			if err != nil {
240				return nil, fmt.Errorf("dns.dot_server: %w", err)
241			}
242			return dnsresolve.NewDoTResolver(host)
243		default:
244			return dnsresolve.NewSystemResolver(), nil
245		}
246	}

31	// use against a malicious or misbehaving DoH server, regardless of any
32	// Content-Length header it claims.

35	// DoHResolver resolves hostnames via DNS-over-HTTPS (RFC 8484): it encodes A
36	// and AAAA queries with golang.org/x/net/dns/dnsmessage, POSTs each to a
37	// configured DoH URL, and extracts the answer addrs and minimum TTL.
38	//
39	// Residual leak: the DoH provider (and any network observer) sees the TLS
40	// SNI of the DoH URL's host and the timing/size of each request — DoH hides
41	// the query *content* from on-path observers, not the fact that this host is
42	// talking to that DoH provider.
43	type DoHResolver struct {
44		url    string
45		client *http.Client
46	}
47	
48	var _ Resolver = (*DoHResolver)(nil)
49	
50	// NewDoHResolver returns a Resolver that POSTs DNS-over-HTTPS queries to
51	// rawURL (which must be an https:// URL). Certificate trust uses the
52	// platform's standard root CA store — there is no production knob to disable
53	// certificate verification.
54	func NewDoHResolver(rawURL string) (*DoHResolver, error) {
55		return newDoHResolver(rawURL, nil)
56	}

59	// overrides the platform trust store with roots, for injecting a test CA
60	// (e.g. from httptest.NewTLSServer) — it exists ONLY for tests in this
61	// package. Production code always goes through NewDoHResolver, which passes
62	// roots=nil and gets tls.Config's default (the system pool).
63	func newDoHResolver(rawURL string, roots *x509.CertPool) (*DoHResolver, error) {
64		parsed, err := url.Parse(rawURL)
65		if err != nil {
66			return nil, fmt.Errorf("dnsresolve: invalid DoH URL %q: %w", rawURL, err)
67		}
68		if parsed.Scheme != "https" {
69			return nil, fmt.Errorf("dnsresolve: DoH URL %q must use https, got scheme %q", rawURL, parsed.Scheme)
70		}
71	
72		transport := &http.Transport{
73			// No proxy surprise: never route DoH queries through an
74			// environment-configured (HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY) proxy — a DoH
75			// resolver silently redirected through an unexpected intermediary
76			// would defeat the point of choosing a specific trusted provider.
77			Proxy: nil,

81			},

83			// TLSClientConfig when this is set explicitly.
84			ForceAttemptHTTP2: true,
85		}
86	
87		return &DoHResolver{
88			url: rawURL,
89			client: &http.Client{
90				Transport: transport,
91				Timeout:   dohTimeout,
92			},
93		}, nil

206:### Optional `[dns]` resolver block
207-
208-Hostname peer endpoints (opt-in per peer with `dns = true`, see the endpoint
209-list note above) are resolved through the OS **system resolver by default** —
210:an absent `[dns]` block is inert. To route that resolution through a private
211:DNS-over-HTTPS or DNS-over-TLS resolver instead, add an explicit `[dns]`
212-block:
213-
214-```toml
215:[dns]
216-resolver = "doh"                                # "system" (default) | "doh" | "dot"
217-doh_url = "https://198.51.100.1/dns-query"       # required iff resolver = "doh"
218-# dot_server = "198.51.100.1"                    # required iff resolver = "dot"
219-# poll_interval = "30s"                          # re-resolution cadence; must be > 0
220-# timeout = "5s"                                 # per-lookup bound; must be > 0
221-```
222-
223:- `[dns]` only **selects the transport** the per-peer `dns = true` opt-in uses;
224-  it never turns hostname resolution on by itself.
225-- **BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant**: `doh_url`/`dot_server`'s host must itself be
226-  reachable *without* a DNS lookup (otherwise resolving your private
227-  resolver's own name would need the very system resolver you configured it
228-  to avoid). Give it as an IP literal, or set `bootstrap_ip` explicitly when
229-  it is a hostname — config load fails fast otherwise.
230-- `dot_server` dials the fixed IANA-assigned DoT port (853); an explicit
231-  `host:port` form must use that exact port.
232-- `doh_url`/`dot_server` are mode-specific: setting the wrong one for the
233-  selected `resolver` (or either one under `resolver = "system"`) is
234-  rejected at load.
235-
236-### 3a. Tuning per-link bandwidth and pacing
237-
238-**Pacing ships DISABLED by default.** When enabled with `pacing_enabled = true`
239-under the `[scheduler]` block, wanbond sizes the per-path send-pace from the
240-bandwidth-delay product (BDP) — the product of each uplink's usable bandwidth and
241-round-trip latency — to bound bufferbloat (excessive queueing) under sustained load.
242-The declared bandwidth is **operator-measured, not auto-tuned**: you measure it once
243-per link and enter it in the config.

fix(config): [dns] loads documented duration strings; wire bootstrap_ip into dial (T72 R2)

2. bootstrap_ip is now wired into the constructed resolver's dial target,
   not just validated: dnsresolve gained NewDoTResolverWithBootstrap
   (dials bootstrap_ip:853, TLS ServerName stays the configured hostname)
   and NewDoHResolverWithBootstrap (pins the TCP connect address via a
   custom Transport.DialContext while TLS SNI/HTTP Host keep the original
   URL host). DNS.NewResolver picks the *WithBootstrap constructor
   whenever bootstrap_ip is set, closing the plaintext-lookup gap the
   BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant (Q33) exists to prevent.

Adds DoTResolver.DialAddr()/DoHResolver.DialHost() accessors so config
and dnsresolve tests can assert the actual dial target, plus hermetic
construct tests (unresolvable hostname + real loopback listener at the
bootstrap address) for both transports.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF

content.text:
### Session summary
- **Did:** addressed both round-2 review criticisms for T72 in the shared checkout, on branch `t72-fix` (based on `implement/T72` @ bb44c9f), since the harness blocked writes to the original `implement/T72` worktree path.
- **Achieved:** pass; `bun`-equivalent gate (`go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` via `nix develop -c`) green; commit `2c5d20390c10e2a0382ad8ecf4a5e616e0b498b1`.
- **Discovered:**
  - go-toml/v2 cannot decode a TOML string into `time.Duration`; the repo's existing pattern (`Path.LinkRTTRaw` + `time.ParseDuration` in `normalize`) was the fix — `DNS.PollInterval`/`Timeout` gained `PollIntervalRaw`/`TimeoutRaw` string counterparts.
  - `dnsresolve.DoTResolver`/`DoHResolver` already had unexported constructor seams (`newDoTResolver(addr, serverName, roots)`, `newDoHResolver(rawURL, roots)`) separating dial target from TLS identity — good seams to build exported bootstrap variants on. For DoH, `http.Transport.DialContext` pins the TCP connect address while net/http still derives SNI/Host from the request URL automatically, so no separate SNI override was needed.
  - Binding to the fixed DoT port 853 needs root, so the DoT hermetic test reuses the unexported seam at an ephemeral port (mirroring what `NewDoTResolverWithBootstrap` does at 853) rather than exercising the exported constructor end-to-end live.
- **Issues:** none outstanding for this task. The actual re-resolution polling loop that will *use* `DNS.PollInterval`/`Timeout`/`NewResolver()` remains separate G5 scope (unchanged from round 1).

Files touched (absolute paths, in `/home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a4c50f2ae82785a2f`):
- `internal/config/dns.go` — `PollIntervalRaw`/`TimeoutRaw` + parsing in `applyDefaults` (now returns `error`); `NewResolver` picks `*WithBootstrap` constructors when `bootstrap_ip` is set.
- `internal/config/config.go` — propagate `DNS.applyDefaults()`'s new error.
- `internal/config/dns_test.go` — updated integer-nanosecond fixtures to the documented string form; added unparseable-duration cases; added `TestDNSDocumentedDurationStringFormLoads`, `TestDNSFullDoHBlockHostnameWithBootstrapIP`, and strengthened the DoT bootstrap test to assert `DialAddr()`.
- `internal/dnsresolve/dot.go` — `NewDoTResolverWithBootstrap`, `DoTResolver.DialAddr()`.
- `internal/dnsresolve/doh.go` — `NewDoHResolverWithBootstrap`, `DoHResolver.DialHost()`, bootstrap-pinned `Transport.DialContext`.
- `internal/dnsresolve/dot_test.go`, `internal/dnsresolve/doh_test.go` — hermetic construct tests (unresolvable hostname + real loopback listener at the bootstrap address) plus stored-dial-target tests.
- `docs/design.md` — one clause noting `bootstrap_ip` is now dialed directly, not just validated.
